explanation: The property states that globally, if the self-test has been completed successfully (selfTestPass) AND configurations have been loaded properly (initDone), then eventually (F) it must be true that the system starts and continues (G) monitoring health parameters (startMonitoring) AND starts and continues reporting health parameters (startReportingHealthParams). This captures the meaning of 'Once X and Y, the system shall start and continue Z'. The 'shall start' implies that the activities begin and persist from that point onward.

The natural language statement specifies a causal relationship: the system must begin monitoring and reporting health parameters "once" two conditions are met: "the self-test has been completed successfully" and "configurations have been loaded properly." The phrase "shall start" implies a mandatory future event. The LTL property `G((selfTestPass & initDone) -> F(startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams))` captures this requirement. The conjunction `selfTestPass & initDone` represents the two prerequisite conditions being met. The `F` operator on `startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams` indicates that these actions will eventually occur. The outer `G` (Globally) operator ensures that this implication holds true at all times; whenever the combined condition of a successful self-test and proper configuration loading is met, it must eventually lead to the system starting to monitor and report health parameters. This formulation appropriately captures the 'whenever-then-eventually' causal structure implied by 'Once X and Y ... the system shall Z'.

The property states that globally, if the self-test has passed successfully ("selfTestPass") and the configurations have been loaded properly ("initDone"), then it must eventually be true that the system starts monitoring health parameters ("startMonitoring") and starts reporting health parameters ("startReportingHealthParams"). This captures the conditional causality expressed by "Once X and Y, the system shall eventually do Z".

The natural language statement specifies a condition that, once met, should lead to certain system behaviors. The condition is that the 'self-test has been completed successfully' (represented by 'selfTestPass') AND 'configurations have been loaded properly' (represented by 'initDone'). Once this combined condition is met, the system 'shall start monitoring' (represented by 'startMonitoring') AND 'reporting health parameters' (represented by 'startReportingHealthParams'). The 'once ... then' phrasing implies an eventual consequence. Therefore, the LTL formula uses a global implication (G(trigger -> F(consequence))). It states that it is always true that if 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' are true, then eventually 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' will both be true.

The property states that globally, if the self-test has passed successfully and the initial configurations have been loaded properly (indicated by 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' being true), then eventually, the system must start monitoring health parameters and start reporting them (indicated by 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' being true). This directly captures the "Once A and B, then C and D shall happen" temporal relationship expressed in the natural language.

The property states that globally, whenever the self-test has been completed successfully (`selfTestPass`) and the configurations have been loaded properly (`initDone`), it must eventually be the case that the system starts monitoring health parameters (`startMonitoring`) and starts reporting health parameters (`startReportingHealthParams`). This accurately captures the conditional 'Once X and Y' leading to the eventual consequence 'Z shall happen'.

The natural language statement specifies a causal relationship: if two conditions are met ('self-test has been completed successfully' AND 'configurations have been loaded properly'), then two specific actions 'start monitoring' AND 'reporting health parameters' must eventually occur. The 'G' (globally) operator ensures that this property holds at all times throughout the system's execution. The '->' (implication) operator establishes the 'if-then' relationship. The conditions 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' are combined with an 'and' operator, meaning both must be true. The 'F' (eventually) operator signifies that the actions 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' (also combined with 'and') will become true at some point in the future, after the conditions are met.

The property states that globally (G), if the self-test has passed successfully (selfTestPass) AND the configurations have been loaded properly (initDone), then it must eventually (F) be true that the system starts monitoring health parameters (startMonitoring) AND starts reporting health parameters (startReportingHealthParams). This captures the 'once... then...' nature, ensuring that the system proceeds to monitoring and reporting after the specified preconditions are met.

The natural language statement implies a sequential dependency: first, two conditions must be met (self-test completion and configuration loading), and then, as a result, the system must eventually start monitoring and reporting. The phrase "Once X ... Y shall Z" indicates that X is a prerequisite that, once met, guarantees Z will eventually follow. The LTL formula `F(selfTestPass & initDone & F(startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams))` captures this. The outer `F` ensures that the combined prerequisite (`selfTestPass` and `initDone`) is eventually met. The `&` operator ensures both `selfTestPass` and `initDone` are true at some point. The inner `F` then asserts that from that point onward, `startMonitoring` and `startReportingHealthParams` will eventually become true. This effectively models the "Once A, then eventually B" temporal relationship for a system startup or transition.

The natural language statement describes a condition (self-test completion and configuration loading) that, once met, should lead to a specific action (monitoring and reporting health parameters). The LTL formula captures this by stating that 'globally' (G), if the 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' (configurations loaded) conditions are met, then it must 'eventually' (F) be true that 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' occur. This ensures that every time the pre-conditions are satisfied, the subsequent actions are guaranteed to happen at some point in the future.
